news_summary: Scientists are making remarkable breakthroughs in AI testing, quantum computing challenges, biological learning in simple organisms, and advanced 3D printing of ultra-hard metals.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques réalisent des avancées remarquables dans les domaines des tests d'intelligence artificielle, des défis liés à l'informatique quantique, de l'apprentissage biologique chez les organismes simples et de l'impression 3D de pointe de métaux ultra-durs.
news_summary_es: Los científicos están logrando avances notables en las pruebas de inteligencia artificial, los retos de la computación cuántica, el aprendizaje biológico en organismos simples y la impresión 3D avanzada de metales ultrarresistentes.

articles.title: Scientists built the hardest AI test ever and the results are surprising
articles.summary: As AI systems began acing traditional tests, researchers realized those benchmarks were no longer tough enough. In response, nearly 1,000 experts created Humanity’s Last Exam, a massive 2,500-question challenge covering highly specialized topics across many fields. The exam was engineered so that any question solvable by current AI models was removed. Early results show even the most advanced systems still struggle — revealing a surprisingly large gap between AI performance and true expert-level knowledge.
articles.content:
As artificial intelligence systems began scoring extremely high on long used academic benchmarks, researchers noticed a growing issue. The tests that once challenged machines were no longer difficult enough. Well known evaluations such as the Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU) exam, which had previously been seen as demanding, now fail to properly measure the capabilities of today's advanced AI models.

To solve this problem, a worldwide group of nearly 1,000 researchers, including a professor from Texas A&M University, developed a new type of test. Their goal was to build an exam that is broad, difficult, and grounded in expert human knowledge in ways that current AI systems still struggle to handle.

The result is "Humanity's Last Exam" (HLE), a 2,500 question assessment covering mathematics, humanities, natural sciences, ancient languages, and a wide range of highly specialized academic fields. Details of the project appear in a paper published in Nature, and additional information about the exam is available at lastexam.ai.

Among the many contributors is Dr. Tung Nguyen, instructional associate professor in the Department of Computer Science and Engineering at Texas A&M. Nguyen helped write and refine many of the exam questions.

"When AI systems start performing extremely well on human benchmarks, it's tempting to think they're approaching human-level understanding," Nguyen said. "But HLE reminds us that intelligence isn't just about pattern recognition -- it's about depth, context and specialized expertise."

The purpose of the exam was not to trick or defeat human test takers. Instead, the goal was to carefully identify areas where AI systems still fall short.

A Global Effort to Measure AI's Limits

Specialists from around the world wrote and reviewed the questions included in Humanity's Last Exam. Each problem was carefully designed so it has one clear, verifiable answer. The questions were also crafted to prevent quick solutions through simple internet searches.

The topics come from advanced academic challenges. Some tasks involve translating ancient Palmyrene inscriptions, while others require identifying tiny anatomical structures in birds or analyzing detailed features of Biblical Hebrew pronunciation.

Researchers tested every question against leading AI systems. If any model was able to answer a question correctly, that question was removed from the final exam. This process ensured the test remained just beyond what current AI systems can reliably solve.

Early testing confirmed that the strategy worked. Even powerful AI models struggled with the exam. GPT-4o achieved a score of 2.7 percent, while Claude 3.5 Sonnet reached 4.1 percent. OpenAI's o1 model performed somewhat better with 8 percent. The most capable systems so far, including Gemini 3.1 Pro and Claude Opus 4.6, have reached accuracy levels between about 40 percent and 50 percent.

Why New AI Benchmarks Are Needed

Nguyen explained that the issue of AI surpassing older tests is more than a technical concern. He contributed 73 of the 2,500 publicly available questions in HLE, the second highest number among contributors, and wrote the most questions related to mathematics and computer science.

"Without accurate assessment tools, policymakers, developers and users risk misinterpreting what AI systems can actually do," he said. "Benchmarks provide the foundation for measuring progress and identifying risks."

According to the research team, high scores on tests originally designed for humans do not necessarily indicate genuine intelligence. Those benchmarks mainly measure how well AI can complete specific tasks created for human learners, rather than capturing deeper understanding.

Not a Threat, but a Tool

Despite the dramatic name, Humanity's Last Exam is not meant to suggest that humans are becoming obsolete. Instead, it highlights the large amount of knowledge and expertise that still remains uniquely human.

"This isn't a race against AI," Nguyen said. "It's a method for understanding where these systems are strong and where they struggle. That understanding helps us build safer, more reliable technologies. And, importantly, it reminds us why human expertise still matters."

Building a Long Term AI Benchmark

Humanity's Last Exam is designed to serve as a durable and transparent benchmark for future AI systems. To support that goal, the researchers have released some questions publicly while keeping the majority hidden so that AI models cannot simply memorize the answers.

"For now, Humanity's Last Exam stands as one of the clearest assessments of the gap between AI and human intelligence," Nguyen said, "and despite rapid technological advances, it remains wide."

A Massive International Research Effort

Nguyen emphasized that the scale of the project demonstrates the value of collaboration across disciplines and countries.

"What made this project extraordinary was the scale," he said. "Experts from nearly every discipline contributed. It wasn't just computer scientists; it was historians, physicists, linguists, medical researchers. That diversity is exactly what exposes the gaps in today's AI systems -- perhaps ironically, it's humans working together."
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant, large-scale international research effort to create a new, highly challenging AI benchmark that reveals the current limitations of AI systems. This contributes to better understanding and safer development of AI technologies, which has broad societal implications. The story focuses on a single, well-detailed topic with clear context and meaningful impact on AI progress and policy.
articles.personality_title: New AI test reveals how far machines are from true expert knowledge
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – As artificial intelligence (AI) gets better, older tests that once measured its skills are no longer hard enough. This made researchers worry that current tests don’t show how much AI really understands.

**What happened** – Nearly 1,000 experts worldwide created a new, tough exam called Humanity’s Last Exam (HLE). It has 2,500 questions on tricky topics like ancient languages, detailed science, and math. The test was carefully made so that any question current AI can answer was taken out. Early results show that even the smartest AI today only gets about 40-50% of the answers right.

**Impact** – This exam is important because it shows where AI still struggles, even though many people think AI is almost as smart as humans. It helps scientists, developers, and leaders understand what AI can and cannot do. The test covers many different fields, making it a strong tool to measure real expert knowledge, not just simple tasks.

**What's next step** – The researchers will keep some questions secret to stop AI from just memorizing answers. They hope HLE will be used for a long time to track AI progress and guide safer, smarter AI development. More experts will likely join to keep improving the exam.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new global test shows AI is still far from mastering expert-level knowledge across many complex subjects.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau test d’IA montre que les machines sont loin de la vraie expertise
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Alors que l’intelligence artificielle (IA) progresse, les anciens tests qui mesuraient ses capacités ne sont plus assez difficiles. Cela a inquiété les chercheurs, car ces tests ne montrent plus ce que l’IA comprend vraiment.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Près de 1 000 experts dans le monde ont créé un nouvel examen très difficile appelé Humanity’s Last Exam (HLE). Il comprend 2 500 questions sur des sujets compliqués comme les langues anciennes, les sciences détaillées et les mathématiques. Les questions que l’IA actuelle pouvait résoudre ont été retirées. Les premiers résultats montrent que même les IA les plus avancées ne répondent correctement qu’à environ 40-50 % des questions.

**Impact** – Cet examen est important car il montre où l’IA a encore des difficultés, alors que beaucoup pensent qu’elle est presque aussi intelligente que les humains. Il aide les scientifiques, développeurs et décideurs à comprendre ce que l’IA peut vraiment faire. Le test couvre de nombreux domaines, ce qui en fait un outil solide pour mesurer une vraie expertise, pas seulement des tâches simples.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs garderont certaines questions secrètes pour empêcher les IA de simplement mémoriser les réponses. Ils espèrent que HLE sera utilisé longtemps pour suivre les progrès de l’IA et guider un développement plus sûr et plus intelligent. D’autres experts devraient rejoindre le projet pour améliorer encore l’examen.

**En une phrase** – Un nouveau test mondial montre que l’IA est encore loin de maîtriser des connaissances expertes dans de nombreux sujets complexes.
articles.personality_title_es: Una nueva prueba de IA revela que las máquinas están lejos del conocimiento experto real
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A medida que la inteligencia artificial (IA) mejora, las pruebas antiguas que antes medían sus habilidades ya no son lo suficientemente difíciles. Esto preocupó a los investigadores porque esas pruebas ya no muestran cuánto entiende realmente la IA.

**Qué pasó** – Casi 1,000 expertos de todo el mundo crearon un nuevo examen muy difícil llamado Humanity’s Last Exam (HLE). Tiene 2,500 preguntas sobre temas complejos como lenguas antiguas, ciencias detalladas y matemáticas. Se eliminaron las preguntas que la IA actual podía responder. Los primeros resultados muestran que incluso las IA más avanzadas solo aciertan entre el 40 y 50 % de las respuestas.

**Impacto** – Este examen es importante porque muestra en qué áreas la IA todavía tiene dificultades, aunque mucha gente piensa que la IA es casi tan inteligente como los humanos. Ayuda a científicos, desarrolladores y líderes a entender lo que la IA realmente puede hacer. El test cubre muchos campos, por lo que es una herramienta fuerte para medir el conocimiento experto real, no solo tareas simples.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores mantendrán algunas preguntas en secreto para evitar que la IA simplemente memorice respuestas. Esperan que HLE se use por mucho tiempo para seguir el progreso de la IA y guiar un desarrollo más seguro y inteligente. Probablemente más expertos se unirán para mejorar el examen.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Una nueva prueba global muestra que la IA todavía está lejos de dominar el conocimiento experto en muchos temas complejos.
articles.image_prompt: An open ancient manuscript glowing softly on a wooden desk surrounded by intricate symbols of mathematics, languages, and sciences gently floating upward like ethereal lanterns, with a diverse circle of silhouetted figures made of interconnected puzzle pieces reaching toward the light, all rendered in warm, earthy tones and delicate brushstrokes.

articles.title: Single-celled organism with no brain is capable of Pavlovian learning
articles.summary: A trumpet-shaped, single-celled organism seems able to predict one thing will follow another, hinting that such associative learning emerged long before multicellular nervous systems
articles.content:
A trumpet-shaped, single-celled organism seems able to predict one thing will follow another, hinting that such associative learning emerged long before multicellular nervous systems

Stentor coeruleus is a single-celled organism with unexpected abilities MELBA PHOTO AGENCY / Alamy

A simple unicellular organism with no brain or neurons seems capable of an advanced form of learning.

The simplest form of learning, known as habituation, is gradually reducing how much you respond to a repeated, harmless stimulus, like a smell or noise. This is common across all animals and has even been seen in plants. It has also been demonstrated in some protists, which have complex eukaryotic cells like animals, land plants and fungi, but are generally single-celled organisms, including the trumpet-shaped Stentor coeruleus and the slime mould Physarum polycephalum.

Much more difficult is learning to connect different types of stimuli or events, and predicting that one is linked to another. Such associative learning was most famously demonstrated when Ivan Pavlov paired the sound of a bell with giving dogs food, resulting in the animals salivating when they heard the bell ring.

Now, Sam Gershman at Harvard University and his colleagues have used similar conditioning experiments to show that Stentor seems capable of associative learning, too.

These surprising organisms live in ponds and swim using lines of hair-like cilia running down their sides. At up to 2 millimetres long, they are giants among single-celled life. At one end, they have an anchor called the holdfast to attach to a surface, while at the other is their trumpet-like feeding apparatus.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Earth Edition Unmissable news about our planet, delivered straight to your inbox each month. Sign up to newsletter

“When they’re attached, they just filter feed. If they are bothered, they’ll quickly contract into a sphere. During that time, they can’t feed, so it’s ecologically advantageous to not respond like that very often unless they have to,” says Gershman.

He and his colleagues used this behaviour to investigate how much Stentor can learn. First, they tapped strongly on the bottom of Petri dishes containing cultures of a few dozen Stentor cells. In response, most of the organisms contracted fast at first, but as the taps continued every 45 seconds, for a total of 60 thuds, fewer and fewer of the Stentor contracted, showing that they had habituated to the signal.

Next, the Stentor cultures felt a weak tap – in response to which fewer of the organisms generally contract – 1 second before a strong tap. The pairs of taps repeated every 45 seconds, which is about how long it takes Stentor to unfurl again.

Over 10 trials of this process, the chance of the organisms contracting immediately after the weak tap first increased and then decreased. “We saw this bump in the graph where the contraction rate initially goes up before going down. If you just present the weak tap by itself, you don’t see this,” says Gershman.

The researchers say this means Stentor has associated the weak tap with the bigger tap, making it the first protist known to be able to master associative learning. “It raises the question of whether apparently simple organisms are capable of aspects of cognition that we generally associate with much more complex, multicellular organisms with brains,” says Gershman.

It also suggests an ancient evolutionary origin of associative learning hundreds of millions of years before the emergence of multicellular nervous systems, he says. Other traces of this may still be seen in the way our neurons seem able to learn from their inputs in a way that isn’t dependent on modifying the synapses or connections between neurons – which is how most learning is thought to work, he says.

“It’s fascinating that a single cell can do such complex things that we thought required a brain, that required neurons, that required behavioural learning,” says Shashank Shekhar at Emory University in Atlanta, Georgia, who has shown that Stentor can aggregate into short-lived groups to feed more efficiently.

He thinks other unicellular organisms may also be capable of associative learning. “My gut feeling is if it’s there once, it’s going to be there more,” he says.

If an organism is learning, that means it must somehow be storing a memory. How this happens in Stentor isn’t yet known, but Gershman suspects it involves receptors that respond to touch by letting calcium flow into the cell, changing the voltage inside and leading Stentor to contract. He suggests that after repeated stimuli, some receptors are being modified somehow, acting as a molecular switch to stop contraction.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery showing that a single-celled organism without a brain can perform associative learning, a complex cognitive behavior previously thought to require multicellular nervous systems. This finding has broad implications for understanding the evolutionary origins of learning and cognition, impacting biology and neuroscience fields. The article provides detailed experimental context and explains the potential mechanisms, making it a substantive and inspiring breakthrough.
articles.personality_title: Single-celled organism learns to predict events without a brain
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long believed that complex learning, like connecting one event to another, requires a brain or nervous system. This type of learning is called associative learning and was first shown in animals like dogs.

**What happened** – Researchers at Harvard studied a single-celled organism named Stentor coeruleus, which has no brain or neurons. They tested if it could learn to connect a weak tap with a strong tap. At first, Stentor contracted when tapped strongly but gradually stopped reacting to repeated taps. When a weak tap was followed by a strong tap several times, Stentor began reacting to the weak tap alone. This shows it learned to predict the strong tap after the weak one.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that even tiny, simple organisms without brains can learn in a way similar to animals with nervous systems. It suggests that associative learning started very early in evolution, long before animals developed brains. Understanding how Stentor learns might help scientists learn more about memory and learning in all living things.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to figure out exactly how Stentor stores memory and changes its behavior. They think it might involve special receptors in the cell that respond to touch and control contraction. Researchers also want to see if other single-celled organisms can learn like Stentor.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A single-celled organism without a brain can learn to predict events, showing that complex learning began long before brains evolved.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un organisme unicellulaire apprend à prévoir des événements sans cerveau
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques pensaient que l’apprentissage complexe, comme relier un événement à un autre, nécessitait un cerveau ou un système nerveux. Ce type d’apprentissage s’appelle l’apprentissage associatif et a été démontré d’abord chez des animaux comme les chiens.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de Harvard ont étudié un organisme unicellulaire appelé Stentor coeruleus, qui n’a ni cerveau ni neurones. Ils ont testé s’il pouvait apprendre à associer un tapotement faible à un tapotement fort. Au début, Stentor se contractait quand on tapait fort, mais il a peu à peu arrêté de réagir aux tapotements répétés. Quand un tapotement faible était suivi d’un tapotement fort plusieurs fois, Stentor a commencé à réagir au tapotement faible seul. Cela montre qu’il a appris à prévoir le tapotement fort après le faible.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que même les organismes très simples sans cerveau peuvent apprendre comme les animaux avec un système nerveux. Cela suggère que l’apprentissage associatif est apparu très tôt dans l’évolution, bien avant que les cerveaux n’existent. Comprendre comment Stentor apprend pourrait aider les scientifiques à mieux comprendre la mémoire et l’apprentissage chez tous les êtres vivants.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent comprendre comment Stentor stocke la mémoire et change son comportement. Ils pensent que cela pourrait impliquer des récepteurs spéciaux qui répondent au toucher et contrôlent la contraction. Ils veulent aussi savoir si d’autres organismes unicellulaires peuvent apprendre comme Stentor.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un organisme unicellulaire sans cerveau peut apprendre à prévoir des événements, montrant que l’apprentissage complexe a commencé bien avant l’évolution des cerveaux.
articles.personality_title_es: Organismo unicelular aprende a predecir eventos sin cerebro
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos pensaban que el aprendizaje complejo, como relacionar un evento con otro, requería un cerebro o sistema nervioso. Este tipo de aprendizaje se llama aprendizaje asociativo y fue demostrado primero en animales como perros.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Harvard estudiaron un organismo unicelular llamado Stentor coeruleus, que no tiene cerebro ni neuronas. Probaron si podía aprender a asociar un toque débil con un toque fuerte. Al principio, Stentor se contraía con toques fuertes, pero poco a poco dejó de reaccionar a toques repetidos. Cuando un toque débil fue seguido por un toque fuerte varias veces, Stentor empezó a reaccionar solo al toque débil. Esto muestra que aprendió a predecir el toque fuerte después del débil.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que incluso organismos simples sin cerebro pueden aprender de forma parecida a animales con sistemas nerviosos. Sugiere que el aprendizaje asociativo apareció muy temprano en la evolución, mucho antes de que existieran los cerebros. Entender cómo aprende Stentor puede ayudar a los científicos a conocer mejor la memoria y el aprendizaje en todos los seres vivos.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren descubrir cómo Stentor guarda la memoria y cambia su comportamiento. Piensan que puede involucrar receptores especiales que responden al tacto y controlan la contracción. También quieren ver si otros organismos unicelulares pueden aprender como Stentor.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un organismo unicelular sin cerebro puede aprender a predecir eventos, mostrando que el aprendizaje complejo comenzó mucho antes de la evolución de los cerebros.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a large, trumpet-shaped single-celled organism with delicate hair-like cilia swimming gently in a softly glowing pond environment, surrounded by subtle, rhythmic waves symbolizing repeated taps, with faint, glowing connections linking smaller and larger ripples to represent associative learning and memory within this solitary microscopic creature.

articles.title: The race to solve the biggest problem in quantum computing
articles.summary: The errors that quantum computers make are holding the technology back. But recent progress in quantum error correction has excited many researchers
articles.content:
The errors that quantum computers make are holding the technology back. But recent progress in quantum error correction has excited many researchers

Quantum computers won’t be truly useful until they can correct their mistakes davide bonaldo / Alamy

Quantum computers are already here, but they make far too many errors. This is arguably the biggest obstacle to the technology really becoming useful, but recent breakthroughs suggest a solution may be on the horizon.

Errors creep into traditional computers too, but there are well-established techniques for correcting them. They rely on redundancy, where extra bits are used to detect when 0s incorrectly swap to 1s or vice versa. In the quantum world, however, it is a lot more challenging.

The laws of quantum mechanics forbid information from being duplicated inside a quantum computer, so redundancy must be achieved by spreading information across groups of qubits – the building blocks of quantum computers – and utilising phenomena that only exist in quantum settings, such as when pairs of particles become linked via quantum entanglement. These qubit groups are called logical qubits and figuring out the optimal way to build and use them is crucial for determining how best to eliminate errors.

A recent surge in progress has made researchers optimistic. “It’s a very exciting time in error correction. For the first time, theory and practice are really making contact,” says Robert Schoelkopf at Yale University.

One of the stumbling blocks for quantum error correction has been that the number of qubits needed to make a logical qubit tends to be large, which makes the whole quantum computer costly and challenging to build. But Xiayu Linpeng at the International Quantum Academy in China and his team have recently demonstrated that this doesn’t have to be the case.

The researchers found that just two superconducting qubits can be combined with a tiny resonator to make one larger qubit that both makes fewer errors and can automatically flag an error when it happens. They then went a step further to show how three such qubits can be grouped together through quantum entanglement for building up computational power without surreptitious errors.

Schoelkopf’s team also recently demonstrated how several operations necessary for quantum computer programs could be implemented with the same type of qubit and exceptionally low error rates, with some errors occurring as rarely as once in a million qubit manipulations.

Even though approaches like this will catch many errors, useful quantum computers will have to contain thousands of logical qubits, meaning some will still creep in. So Arian Vezvaee at start-up Quantum Elements and his colleagues have tested a way to add further error protection to logical qubits, like wearing a raincoat under an umbrella.

The key idea is to not let any qubits sit idle for too long, as that makes them lose their special quantum properties and become corrupted. The team showed that giving idle qubits extra “kicks” of electromagnetic radiation can create the most reliable entanglement between logical qubits to date.

The exact recipe for how to combine physical qubits into logical ones really matters for some of the most precise calculations, as David Muñoz Ramo at quantum computing firm Quantinuum and his colleagues found when investigating an algorithm that determines the lowest possible energy that a hydrogen molecule can have. There, the precision needed is so high that basic error-correcting methods aren’t enough.

Such innovation in error-correcting programs will be crucial for the success or failure of quantum computers, says James Wootton at start-up Moth Quantum. “We’re still in a phase where researchers are learning how all the pieces of error correction fit together.” Quantum computers can’t yet operate effectively without errors, but we are starting to see the engineering foundations of this appear, he says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports significant recent breakthroughs in quantum error correction, a critical challenge holding back the practical use of quantum computers. These advances have broad implications for the future of computing technology, potentially enabling powerful new applications and benefits for society. The article provides detailed context on the scientific progress and its importance, making it a substantive and inspiring technology development.
articles.personality_title: New breakthroughs bring quantum computers closer to fixing their own errors
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum computers are powerful machines that work differently from regular computers. But they often make mistakes, called errors, which stop them from working well. Fixing these errors has been very hard because quantum information cannot be copied like normal data.

**What happened** – Recently, scientists have made important progress in a method called quantum error correction. They found ways to combine small groups of quantum bits, or qubits, to detect and fix errors automatically. For example, a team in China showed that just two qubits and a small device can create a stronger qubit that catches mistakes. Other researchers improved how these qubits work together with fewer errors, sometimes as low as one error in a million operations.

**Impact** – These discoveries are important because they show quantum computers can become more reliable without needing huge numbers of qubits. This makes building useful quantum computers more possible and less expensive. Also, better error correction helps with very precise calculations, like predicting how molecules behave, which regular computers struggle to do.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue to find better ways to combine and protect qubits, making quantum computers even more accurate. They will test these methods on bigger machines with thousands of qubits. This work will help quantum computers solve real-world problems in science, medicine, and technology.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New advances in correcting quantum computer errors are paving the way for reliable and powerful quantum machines in the near future.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles avancées rapprochent les ordinateurs quantiques de la correction automatique de leurs erreurs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ordinateurs quantiques sont des machines puissantes qui fonctionnent différemment des ordinateurs classiques. Mais ils font souvent des erreurs, appelées fautes, qui les empêchent de bien fonctionner. Corriger ces erreurs est difficile car l’information quantique ne peut pas être copiée comme les données normales.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Récemment, des scientifiques ont fait des progrès importants dans une méthode appelée correction d’erreurs quantiques. Ils ont trouvé des moyens de combiner de petits groupes de bits quantiques, ou qubits, pour détecter et corriger automatiquement les erreurs. Par exemple, une équipe en Chine a montré que deux qubits et un petit dispositif peuvent créer un qubit plus solide qui détecte les fautes. D’autres chercheurs ont amélioré la collaboration entre ces qubits avec moins d’erreurs, parfois aussi rares qu’une erreur sur un million d’opérations.

**Impact** – Ces découvertes sont importantes car elles montrent que les ordinateurs quantiques peuvent devenir plus fiables sans avoir besoin d’un très grand nombre de qubits. Cela rend la construction d’ordinateurs quantiques utiles plus possible et moins coûteuse. De plus, une meilleure correction d’erreurs aide pour des calculs très précis, comme prédire le comportement des molécules, ce que les ordinateurs classiques ont du mal à faire.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à trouver de meilleures façons de combiner et protéger les qubits, rendant les ordinateurs quantiques encore plus précis. Ils testeront ces méthodes sur des machines plus grandes avec des milliers de qubits. Ce travail aidera les ordinateurs quantiques à résoudre des problèmes réels en science, médecine et technologie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De nouvelles avancées dans la correction des erreurs des ordinateurs quantiques ouvrent la voie à des machines fiables et puissantes prochainement.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos avances acercan a las computadoras cuánticas a corregir sus propios errores
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las computadoras cuánticas son máquinas potentes que funcionan diferente a las computadoras normales. Pero cometen muchos errores, llamados fallos, que impiden que funcionen bien. Corregir estos errores es difícil porque la información cuántica no se puede copiar como los datos comunes.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, científicos lograron avances importantes en un método llamado corrección de errores cuánticos. Encontraron formas de combinar pequeños grupos de bits cuánticos, o qubits, para detectar y corregir errores automáticamente. Por ejemplo, un equipo en China demostró que solo dos qubits y un pequeño dispositivo pueden crear un qubit más fuerte que detecta fallos. Otros investigadores mejoraron cómo trabajan juntos estos qubits con menos errores, a veces tan bajos como uno en un millón de operaciones.

**Impacto** – Estos descubrimientos son importantes porque muestran que las computadoras cuánticas pueden ser más confiables sin necesitar una gran cantidad de qubits. Esto hace que construir computadoras cuánticas útiles sea más posible y menos caro. Además, una mejor corrección de errores ayuda con cálculos muy precisos, como predecir cómo se comportan las moléculas, algo que las computadoras normales no pueden hacer bien.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán buscando mejores formas de combinar y proteger los qubits para hacer las computadoras cuánticas aún más precisas. Probarán estos métodos en máquinas más grandes con miles de qubits. Este trabajo ayudará a las computadoras cuánticas a resolver problemas reales en ciencia, medicina y tecnología.

**Resumen en una frase** – Nuevos avances en la corrección de errores de computadoras cuánticas están preparando el camino para máquinas confiables y potentes en un futuro cercano.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of glowing interconnected orbs representing qubits linked by delicate, shimmering threads of quantum entanglement, with some orbs gently enveloped by translucent protective layers symbolizing error correction, set against a soft, natural gradient background of blues and warm earth tones conveying hope and innovation in quantum computing.

articles.title: Scientists just found a way to 3D print one of the hardest metals on Earth
articles.summary: Scientists have found a promising new way to manufacture one of industry’s toughest materials—tungsten carbide–cobalt—using advanced 3D printing. Normally, producing this ultra-hard material requires high-pressure processes that waste large amounts of expensive tungsten and cobalt. The new approach uses a hot-wire laser technique that softens the metals rather than fully melting them, allowing manufacturers to deposit the material only where it’s needed.
articles.content:
Tungsten carbide-cobalt (WC-Co) is widely valued for its extreme hardness, but that same strength also makes it very difficult to shape and manufacture. Current production methods consume large amounts of costly material while delivering relatively modest yields. As a result, researchers have been searching for a more efficient and economical way to produce these exceptionally tough materials.

WC-Co cemented carbides are essential for applications that demand strong resistance to wear and high hardness, including cutting and construction tools. Traditionally, these materials are produced through powder metallurgy. In this process, powders of WC and Co are compressed under high pressure and heated in sintering machines to form solid cemented carbide. While this method produces very durable final products, it uses significant quantities of expensive raw materials and generates inefficient yields.

To address this issue, researchers explored a different approach using additive manufacturing (AM, also commonly known as 3D printing). Their work also incorporates a technique called hot-wire laser irradiation. Together, these methods aim to create cemented carbides that retain their strength and durability while reducing both material waste and production costs.

The findings were published in the International Journal of Refractory Metals and Hard Materials and are scheduled to appear in the journal's April 2026 print issue.

Laser-Based Additive Manufacturing Approach

The study examined additive manufacturing using hot-wire laser irradiation and tested two different fabrication strategies. Hot-wire laser irradiation (also called laser hot-wire welding) combines a laser beam with a heated filler wire. This pairing increases the deposition rate (how much filler metal is added) and improves overall manufacturing efficiency.

In one of the experimental approaches, the cemented carbide rod leads the direction of fabrication while the laser directly irradiates the top of the rod. In the second approach, the laser leads the process and directs energy between the bottom of the cemented carbide rod and the base material (iron). In both techniques, the materials are softened during fabrication rather than fully melted to form the cemented carbide structure.

"Cemented carbides are extremely hard materials used for cutting tool edges and similar applications, but they are made from very expensive raw materials such as tungsten and cobalt, making reduction of material usage highly desirable. By using additive manufacturing, cemented carbide can be deposited only where it is needed, thereby reducing material consumption," said corresponding author Keita Marumoto, assistant professor at Hiroshima University's Graduate School of Advanced Science and Engineering.

Achieving Defect-Free Industrial Hardness

The experiments showed that this additive manufacturing strategy can preserve the hardness and mechanical strength typically achieved through conventional manufacturing methods. The resulting material reached hardness levels above 1400 HV (a unit representing resistance to penetration) while avoiding defects or material breakdown.

Materials with this level of hardness are among the toughest used in industrial applications and rank just below superhard materials such as sapphire and diamond. Producing cemented carbide molds without defects appears achievable with this approach, which was the primary objective of the research. However, the results varied depending on the fabrication method used.

For instance, the rod-leading technique led to decomposition of WC near the top portion of the build, which created defects in the finished material. The laser-leading method also struggled to maintain the hardness required for success.

Researchers addressed these issues by introducing a nickel alloy-based intermediate layer. Combined with careful control of temperature conditions (above the melting point for cobalt, below the temperature of grain growth), this adjustment enabled the production of cemented carbide using additive manufacturing while preserving the material's hardness.

Future Improvements and Applications

The results provide a promising starting point for further development. Future work will focus on reducing cracking during fabrication and enabling the creation of more complex shapes.

"The approach of forming metal materials by softening them rather than fully melting them is novel, and it has the potential to be applied not only to cemented carbides, which were the focus of this study, but also to other materials," said Marumoto.

Looking ahead, researchers aim to fabricate cutting tools, investigate the use of other materials, and continue studying ways to improve the durability of parts made with this technique.

Keita Marumoto and Motomichi Yamamoto of the Graduate School of Advanced Science and Engineering at Hiroshima University and Takashi Abe, Keigo Nagamori, Hiroshi Ichikawa and Akio Nishiyama of the Mitsubishi Materials Hardmetal Corporation contributed to this research.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in manufacturing ultra-hard materials using advanced 3D printing techniques that reduce waste and cost while preserving material properties. This innovation has broad industrial applications, including cutting and construction tools, and represents a meaningful improvement over traditional methods, with detailed context and future development plans.
articles.personality_title: Scientists develop new 3D printing method for ultra-hard tungsten carbide materials
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Tungsten carbide-cobalt (WC-Co) is one of the hardest materials used in tools and construction. However, making it is expensive and wasteful because traditional methods require high pressure and heat, which use a lot of costly tungsten and cobalt metals.

**What happened** – Researchers from Hiroshima University and Mitsubishi Materials Hardmetal Corporation created a new way to make WC-Co using 3D printing combined with a hot-wire laser technique. Instead of fully melting the metals, this method softens them to build the material only where needed. They tested two ways of applying the laser and added a nickel alloy layer to keep the material strong and free of defects.

**Impact** – This new method keeps the material’s extreme hardness, similar to traditional production, but uses less raw material and produces fewer defects. It can make tough, precise shapes for cutting tools and other industrial uses more efficiently and cheaply.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to improve the process by reducing cracks and creating more complex shapes. They also want to try this softening technique on other materials and build actual cutting tools using the new method.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have developed a 3D printing technique that can produce ultra-hard tungsten carbide materials with less waste and high quality, promising better and cheaper industrial tools.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques inventent une nouvelle méthode d'impression 3D pour les matériaux ultra-durs en carbure de tungstène
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le carbure de tungstène-cobalt (WC-Co) est l'un des matériaux les plus durs utilisés dans les outils et la construction. Cependant, sa fabrication est coûteuse et génère beaucoup de déchets, car les méthodes traditionnelles nécessitent une haute pression et de la chaleur, consommant beaucoup de tungstène et de cobalt.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université d'Hiroshima et de Mitsubishi Materials Hardmetal Corporation ont créé un nouveau procédé utilisant l'impression 3D combinée à une technique laser à fil chaud. Au lieu de fondre complètement les métaux, cette méthode les ramollit pour construire le matériau uniquement là où c'est nécessaire. Ils ont testé deux façons d'appliquer le laser et ajouté une couche d'alliage de nickel pour garder la solidité et éviter les défauts.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle méthode conserve la dureté extrême du matériau, similaire à la production traditionnelle, mais utilise moins de matière première et produit moins de défauts. Elle permet de fabriquer des formes précises et résistantes pour les outils de coupe et autres usages industriels de manière plus efficace et économique.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs souhaitent améliorer le procédé en réduisant les fissures et en créant des formes plus complexes. Ils veulent aussi appliquer cette technique de ramollissement à d'autres matériaux et fabriquer des outils de coupe réels.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont développé une technique d'impression 3D capable de produire des matériaux ultra-durs en carbure de tungstène avec moins de déchets et une haute qualité, promettant des outils industriels meilleurs et moins chers.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos desarrollan nuevo método de impresión 3D para materiales ultra duros de carburo de tungsteno
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El carburo de tungsteno-cobalto (WC-Co) es uno de los materiales más duros usados en herramientas y construcción. Sin embargo, su fabricación es cara y genera mucho desperdicio porque los métodos tradicionales requieren alta presión y calor, usando mucho tungsteno y cobalto.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Hiroshima y Mitsubishi Materials Hardmetal Corporation crearon una nueva forma de fabricar WC-Co usando impresión 3D combinada con una técnica láser de alambre caliente. En lugar de fundir completamente los metales, este método los suaviza para construir el material solo donde se necesita. Probaron dos formas de aplicar el láser y añadieron una capa de aleación de níquel para mantener la fuerza y evitar defectos.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo método mantiene la extrema dureza del material, similar a la producción tradicional, pero usa menos materia prima y produce menos defectos. Puede fabricar formas duras y precisas para herramientas de corte y otros usos industriales de manera más eficiente y económica.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean mejorar el proceso reduciendo grietas y creando formas más complejas. También quieren aplicar esta técnica de suavizado a otros materiales y fabricar herramientas de corte reales.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos han desarrollado una técnica de impresión 3D que puede producir materiales ultra duros de carburo de tungsteno con menos desperdicio y alta calidad, prometiendo herramientas industriales mejores y más baratas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, intricately layered metal rod being gently shaped by a fine, radiant laser beam combined with a softly heated wire, surrounded by abstract representations of tungsten and cobalt atoms bonding tightly, symbolizing the innovative 3D printing process creating one of the hardest metals on Earth with precision and care.
